TEXT.BibTeX_citation: @misc{Wiki_szy_nipaluma-hucang,_hocang, title = nipaluma-hucang,_hocang, year = 2025, month = May, day = 5, note = {In Wikipedia [Sakizaya]}, url = {https://szy.wikipedia.org/wiki/nipaluma-hucang,_hocang} }

S.FORM: == hucang, hocang == ËôéÊùñ u kakuniza salaedan :Ê§çÁâ©Áïå Plantae panan :Ë¢´Â≠êÊ§çÁâ©ÈñÄ Magnoliophyta upiz :ÈõôÂ≠êËëâÊ§çÁâ©Á∂± Magnoliopsida mata :Áü≥Á´πÁõÆ Caryophyllales sapamat :ËìºÁßë Polygonaceae mikitinay :‰ΩïÈ¶ñÁÉèÂ±¨ Fallopia hicahicaay :ËôéÊùñ F. japonica u siwkay nu nipaluma u sanek nu nipaluma malaheci tu imelang pasubana i cacudadan a lacul u zuma a cidekay a kamu, saungay misapayu' a cidekay u sulit nu zuma a kamu-Hulam atu Amilika Fallopia japonica, synonym Reynoutria japonica, commonly known as Asian knotweed or Japanese knotweed, is a large, herbaceous perennial plant of the knotweed and buckwheat family Polygonaceae. It is native to East Asia in Japan, China and Korea. In North America and Europe, the species has successfully established in numerous habitats and is classified as an invasive species in several countries. Japanese knotweed has hollow stems with distinct raised nodes that give it the appearance of bamboo, though it is not related. While stems may reach a maximum height of 3‚Äì4 m (9.8‚Äì13.1 ft) each growing season, it is typical to see much smaller plants in places where they sprout through cracks in the pavement or are repeatedly cut down. The leaves are broad oval with a truncated base, 7‚Äì14 cm (2.8‚Äì5.5 in) long and 5‚Äì12 cm (2.0‚Äì4.7 in) broad, with an entire margin. The flowers are small, cream or white, produced in erect racemes 6‚Äì15 cm (2.4‚Äì5.9 in) long in late summer and early autumn. Closely related species include giant knotweed (Fallopia sachalinensis, syn. Polygonum sachalinense) and Russian vine (Fallopia baldschuanica, Polygonum baldschuanicum). Other English names for Japanese knotweed include fleeceflower, Himalayan fleece vine, monkeyweed, monkey fungus, Hancock's curse, elephant ears, pea shooters, donkey rhubarb , sally rhubarb, Japanese bamboo, American bamboo, and Mexican bamboo . In Chinese medicine, it is known as Huzhang (Chinese: ËôéÊùñ; pinyin: H«îzh√Ýng), which translates to "tiger stick". There are also regional names, and it is sometimes confused with sorrel. In Japanese, the name is itadori .

¶…ôa…ô mikitinaj :**** far~…ær~…æopia ƒßi ¶aƒßi ¶aaj :** f. japoni ¶a u siwkaj nu nipar~…æuma u san…ôk nu nipar~…æuma mar~…æaƒß…ô ¶i tu im…ôr~…æa≈ã pasubana i ¶a ¶udadan a r~…æa ¶ur~…æ u zuma a ¶id…ôkaj a kamu, sau≈ãaj misapaju î~ ° a ¶id…ôkaj u sur~…æit nu zuma a kamu-ƒßur~…æam atu amir~…æika far~…ær~…æopia japoni ¶a, sjnonjm *…ônoutria japoni ¶a, ¶ommonr~…æj known as asian knotw…ô…ôd or *apan…ôs…ô knotw…ô…ôd, is a r~…æarg…ô, ƒß…ôrba ¶…ôous p…ôr…ônniar~…æ pr~…æant of tƒß…ô knotw…ô…ôd and bu ¶kwƒß…ôat famir~…æj por~…æjgona ¶…ôa…ô. it is nati*…ô to …ôast asia in *apan, ¶ƒßina and kor…ôa. in nortƒß am…ôri ¶a and …ôurop…ô, tƒß…ô sp…ô ¶i…ôs ƒßas su ¶ ¶…ôssfur~…ær~…æj …ôstabr~…æisƒß…ôd in num…ôrous ƒßabitats and is ¶r~…æassifi…ôd as an in*asi*…ô sp…ô ¶i…ôs in s…ô*…ôrar~…æ ¶ountri…ôs. *apan…ôs…ô knotw…ô…ôd ƒßas ƒßor~…ær~…æow st…ôms witƒß distin ¶t rais…ôd nod…ôs tƒßat gi*…ô it tƒß…ô app…ôaran ¶…ô of bamboo, tƒßougƒß it is not r…ôr~…æat…ôd. wƒßir~…æ…ô st…ôms maj r…ôa ¶ƒß a ma*imum ƒß…ôigƒßt of *** m (*.****.* ft) …ôa ¶ƒß growi≈ã s…ôason, it is tjpi ¶ar~…æ to s…ô…ô mu ¶ƒß smar~…ær~…æ…ôr pr~…æants in pr~…æa ¶…ôs wƒß…ôr…ô tƒß…ô sprout tƒßrougƒß ¶ra ¶ks in tƒß…ô pa*…ôm…ônt or ar…ô r…ôp…ôat…ôdr~…æj ¶ut down. tƒß…ô r~…æ…ôa*…ôs ar…ô broad o*ar~…æ witƒß a trun ¶at…ôd bas…ô, **** ¶m (*.***.* in) r~…æo≈ã and **** ¶m (*.***.* in) broad, witƒß an …ôntir…ô margin. tƒß…ô fr~…æow…ôrs ar…ô smar~…ær~…æ, ¶r…ôam or wƒßit…ô, produ ¶…ôd in …ôr…ô ¶t ra ¶…ôm…ôs **** ¶m (*.***.* in) r~…æo≈ã in r~…æat…ô summ…ôr and …ôarr~…æj autumn. ¶r~…æos…ôr~…æj r…ôr~…æat…ôd sp…ô ¶i…ôs in ¶r~…æud…ô giant knotw…ô…ôd (far~…ær~…æopia sa ¶ƒßar~…æin…ônsis, sjn. por~…æjgonum sa ¶ƒßar~…æin…ôns…ô) and *ussian *in…ô (far~…ær~…æopia bar~…æds ¶ƒßuani ¶a, por~…æjgonum bar~…æds ¶ƒßuani ¶um). otƒß…ôr …ô≈ãr~…æisƒß nam…ôs for *apan…ôs…ô knotw…ô…ôd in ¶r~…æud…ô fr~…æ…ô…ô ¶…ôfr~…æow…ôr, ƒßimar~…æajan fr~…æ…ô…ô ¶…ô *in…ô, monk…ôw…ô…ôd, monk…ô fu≈ãus, ƒßan ¶o ¶k î~ °s ¶urs…ô, …ôr~…æ…ôpƒßant …ôars, p…ôa sƒßoot…ôrs, donk…ô rƒßubarb , sar~…ær~…æj rƒßubarb, *apan…ôs…ô bamboo, am…ôri ¶an bamboo, and m…ô*i ¶an bamboo . in ¶ƒßin…ôs…ô m…ôdi ¶in…ô, it is known as ƒßuzƒßa≈ã ( ¶ƒßin…ôs…ô: **; pinjin: ƒß*zƒß*≈ã), wƒßi ¶ƒß transr~…æat…ôs to "tig…ôr sti ¶k". tƒß…ôr…ô ar…ô ar~…æso r…ôgionar~…æ nam…ôs, and it is som…ôtim…ôs ¶onfus…ôd witƒß sorr…ôr~…æ. in *apan…ôs…ô, tƒß…ô nam…ô is itadori .
S.FORM: == hucang, hocang == ËôéÊùñ u kakuniza salaedan :Ê§çÁâ©Áïå Plantae panan :Ë¢´Â≠êÊ§çÁâ©ÈñÄ Magnoliophyta upiz :ÈõôÂ≠êËëâÊ§çÁâ©Á∂± Magnoliopsida mata :Áü≥Á´πÁõÆ Caryophyllales sapamat :ËìºÁßë Polygonaceae mikitinay :‰ΩïÈ¶ñÁÉèÂ±¨ Fallopia hicahicaay :ËôéÊùñ F. japonica u siwkay nu nipaluma u sanek nu nipaluma malaheci tu imelang pasubana i cacudadan a lacul u zuma a cidekay a kamu, saungay misapayu' a cidekay u sulit nu zuma a kamu-Hulam atu Amilika Fallopia japonica, synonym Reynoutria japonica, commonly known as Asian knotweed or Japanese knotweed, is a large, herbaceous perennial plant of the knotweed and buckwheat family Polygonaceae. It is native to East Asia in Japan, China and Korea. In North America and Europe, the species has successfully established in numerous habitats and is classified as an invasive species in several countries. Japanese knotweed has hollow stems with distinct raised nodes that give it the appearance of bamboo, though it is not related. While stems may reach a maximum height of 3‚Äì4 m (9.8‚Äì13.1 ft) each growing season, it is typical to see much smaller plants in places where they sprout through cracks in the pavement or are repeatedly cut down. The leaves are broad oval with a truncated base, 7‚Äì14 cm (2.8‚Äì5.5 in) long and 5‚Äì12 cm (2.0‚Äì4.7 in) broad, with an entire margin. The flowers are small, cream or white, produced in erect racemes 6‚Äì15 cm (2.4‚Äì5.9 in) long in late summer and early autumn. Closely related species include giant knotweed (Fallopia sachalinensis, syn. Polygonum sachalinense) and Russian vine (Fallopia baldschuanica, Polygonum baldschuanicum). Other English names for Japanese knotweed include fleeceflower, Himalayan fleece vine, monkeyweed, monkey fungus, Hancock's curse, elephant ears, pea shooters, donkey rhubarb , sally rhubarb, Japanese bamboo, American bamboo, and Mexican bamboo . In Chinese medicine, it is known as Huzhang (Chinese: ËôéÊùñ; pinyin: H«îzh√Ýng), which translates to "tiger stick". There are also regional names, and it is sometimes confused with sorrel. In Japanese, the name is itadori .
